source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 4.4.4-4.4.9 mkdir/touch/rmdir/rm/mv/cp (pp. 45-50)

questions.q: Create the directory tree /srv/data/2026, creating any parent directories that do not yet exist.
questions.why: mkdir needs each parent to already exist; -p creates the missing parent directories along the way.

questions.q: Create an empty file called report in the current directory.
questions.why: touch updates a file's timestamp, or creates it empty if it does not exist - its most common use.
rmdir removes an empty directory; a non-empty one needs rm -r instead.

questions.q: You try 'rmdir project' but it fails because the directory is not empty. What is the guide's remedy?
options: Use rm -r to delete the directory and its contents
questions.why: rmdir only removes empty directories; deleting a non-empty directory and its contents requires 'rm -r'.

questions.q: Delete the directory olddir together with all of its files and subdirectories.
questions.why: -r deletes a directory recursively; -f additionally suppresses the confirmation prompt.

questions.q: On a Red Hat / Rocky system, why does plain 'rm file' ask for confirmation when the manual says rm does not?
options: Because rm is aliased to 'rm -i' on these distributions
Because rm always asks on every Linux
Because the file is owned by root
questions.why: On Red Hat/Rocky, rm is an alias for 'rm -i', so it prompts; other distributions such as Debian give no prompt by default.

questions.q: Rename the file draft to final in the current directory.
questions.why: mv both moves and renames; with two names in the same directory it renames draft to final.

questions.q: Copy the directory /home/rockstar and everything inside it into /tmp.
questions.why: cp copies files; -r is required to copy a directory along with its files and subdirectories.

questions.q: Which cp option keeps the original owner, permissions and timestamp on the copy?
questions.why: -p preserves owner, permissions and timestamp; -s would instead create a symbolic link rather than a copy.
